nameMeanings.name: Lequier
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.

variants.name: Lecuyer
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Alcide, Jacques, Marcel, Alain, Armand, Aurele, Emile, Laure, Lucienne, Michel, Monique, Pierre.

French (Lécuyer): status name, with fused definite article l', from écuyer ‘squire’, a term originally meaning ‘shield-bearer’ (from Latin scutarius, a derivative of scutum ‘shield’). This became a status name for a man belonging to the social rank immediately below that of knight. In North America the surname was sometimes translated into English as Cook ; the original term écuyer having lost transparency of meaning, it was taken as a noun derivative of the verb cuire ‘to cook’. Compare Lagoy and Lequire .

History: This surname is listed in the (US) National Huguenot Society's register of qualified Huguenot ancestors (in the forms L'Écuyer, L'Escuyer, L'Esquier, and Lequer) and also in the similar register of the Huguenot Society of America (in the form L'Escuyer).

nameMeanings.name: Marie
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: form of Maria . When first introduced to England in the Middle Ages, it was Anglicized in pronunciation and respelled Mary . This French form was reintroduced into the English-speaking world as a separate name in the 19th century, and is still pronounced more or less in the French manner, although sometimes with the stress on the first syllable. It is now often used in combination with other names such as Ellie, Chloe, and Lisa.
